description: Apollo 12 Flight Journal - Day 2, part 2: Quiet end to the day

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
Although we are looking at a relatively blank Flight Plan for the remainder of the evening and expect a quiet evening, we will leave the release line up live at least for a while.
If we continue to have long intervals between conversations with the crew, we will take the release line down and play back the tapes at periodic intervals.
At 31 hours, 37 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Roger.
Battery vent is - Battery compartment has been vented and we started charging Bat A at 31:37.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we just got a Master Alarm with nothing on the panel.
Got any idea what that might have been?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Stand by, 12.
We'll start checking.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
We don't see much out of the ordinary, Pete.
We are on a low bit rate right now, and we will be in high bit rate shortly where we can look at things a lot more closely.
About the only suspicious item is your O2flow rate is just a shade high, but we see nothing else.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
We're venting the - the urine system - that new one, and we're just letting it run.
I'll go shut her off.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Looking at the Earth down there, Houston, it looks like you are littler than a golf ball at arm's length now.
Sure looks pretty though.
The terminator looks like it's - it's kind of hard to tell from this distance - looks like it's passing somewhere just west of Tallahassee.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
It's just getting dark out here now.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
Your accumulator cycled just about the time you got that Master Alarm, so it may have been an O2flow high, and you may have just had a flicker on your matrix panel.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; at 31 hours, 48 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 12.
We currently read a velocity on Apollo 12 at 4,214 feet per second [1,284 m/s].
Its present altitude, 119,204 nautical miles [220,766 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Well, Houston, we have settled down to a normal routine during the day.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Music - 'Suspicious Minds' by Elvis Presley.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, 12.
We'll have some scores for you shortly.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Do you read 12?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Reading you loud and clear on voice.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Roger.
Did you get the transmission a little while ago?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We got a few words and a little bit of music, and then it quit.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
I think you lost our antenna about then.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're trying all these things we didn't have in Gemini, like toothpaste and shaving and - We are really having a ball up here.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: All dressed up and no place to go.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Oh, we're going someplace.
We can see it getting bigger and bigger all the time.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
I have a little sports news for you.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
The Phoenix 200 is still in a hold status; they apparently couldn't get past the thunderstorms.
And here are some scores.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We were...
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: ...Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: No, go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.

Some scores: Texas Tech 41, Baylor 7 - these are all finals - Texas 69, TCU 7; Arkansas 28, SMU 5; Rice 7, the Aggies 6; Northwestern 30, Indiana 27; Michigan 51, Iowa 6; Wisconsin 55, Illinois 14; Ole Miss 38, Tennessee 0; Missouri 40 and Iowa State 13.
And the scores on the West Coast are starting to come in now.
Dick, you'll be happy to hear Washington and Southern Cal are tied 7 to 7 in the third quarter, and Oregon is - in the third quarter - is ahead of oh - UCLA 10 to 7.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Jerry, I - makes me happy but I'll bet it doesn't make you happy, does it?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Game's not over, Dick.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We've been trying to look at the United States through the monocular, and it just looks like most of the States is covered with clouds.
Are you having bad weather generally over the country?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We'll have to take a look at an overall map, but here in Houston, it's been CAVU all day and beautiful, a wonderful, clear fall day.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: 12, Houston.
Dick, I got bad news for you.
A final: USC 16, Washington 7.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Boy, you sure changed that in a hurry, didn't you.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I didn't want to give you too long to gloat over that one.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I think you were sandbagging me, Jerry.
I was about to bet you.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I should've waited.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
We currently show Apollo 12 at an altitude of 119,954 nautical miles [222,155 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 12, Houston.
I've got a weather map here for the United States.
All up through the northeast part of the country it looks like it's either overcast or broken.
And then in the Southeast and in the South and up through about the panhandle of Texas down into Florida it's all clear.
And then, moving on further west, you get into New Mexico, Arizona, California, and you begin to pick up overcast skies again, all the way from Montana all the way down to Arizona.
Montana has got a few broken and scattered clouds in the eastern side and then it's pretty bad over on the western side.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Yes, we - we could - We were having a hard time picking out exactly where we were looking at.

Even with the monocular everything is now - tends to be brown - and having a hard time picking out the land from the water, but it seems like that part of the country that we can see is pretty well covered with clouds.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger.
It looks like the whole West Coast is socked in [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: 12, Houston.
The weather system is a great big high sitting down over, oh, Gulfport - Biloxi area, so that's why we are so clear down in the Southeast.
We have a weak high up around the four corners area of Utah, Colorado, New Mexico, Arizona.
And then everywhere else is just bad news.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I see.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: How are our families doing, Jerry?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Haven't talked to them, but I'll make a few calls and give you some answers.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
Appreciate that every day if you could.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We'll do her.
Everybody is probably at supper right now, so probably catch them all at home.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
We are at 32 hours, 10 minutes into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston.
We are just looking out of the window now and we were all talking about old project FSMOE.
You remember that?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I guess that one doesn't compute, 12.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Say again.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: That one doesn't compute.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Project FSMOE?
Full-Scale Mock-up Of Earth?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Hard to tell which one we've got out the window.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Is it blue or gray?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Pretty blue and white [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Hey, Jer, somebody's probably already said this before, but - That place looks like an oasis down there.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger, Dick.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Dick is referring to Jim Lovell's statement on Apollo 8.
At085:46:23 GETduring their penultimate orbit of the Moon, Lovell says "The vast loneliness up here of the Moon is awe inspiring, and it makes you realize just what you have back there on Earth.
The Earth from here is a grand oasis in the big vastness of space."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 32 hours, 17 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 12.
The Apollo 12 spacecraft at this time 120,348 nautical miles [222,884 km] above the Earth.
It is now traveling at 4,172 feet per second [1,272 m/s].
At this time we will take down the live release line and come up at periodic intervals with taped replays.
We expect relatively quiet period with regard to conversation between our capsule communicator, Jerry Carr, and the crew.
At 32 hours, 18 minutes into the flight of Apollo 12; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 32 hours, 42 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 12.
Since our last report, we have had no further conversation with the Apollo 12 crew, no doubt who are relaxing at this time after an active day and half in space.
Mission Control Center now at this time, they - what would appear to be a somewhat powered-down mode?
Very little activity aside from the individual flight controllers looking at their displays; perhaps one or two having sandwiches being brought in to them.
At present we show an altitude above the Earth for Apollo 12 at 121,393 nautical miles [224,820 km].
Apollo 12's velocity now reads 4,134 feet per second [1,260 m/s].
And at 32 hours, 43 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: What's your preliminary look at the midcourse show?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Stand by a second, Pete.
While they're ginning up the answer, got some nice warm words for you on your SPS burn.
Evaluation shows that your ISP is within one tenth of a second, and your thrust is within 20 pounds on that engine so you got a real hummer there.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Very good.
I guess we can thank Mr.
Thibodaux [NASA propulsion expert] for that one.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Joseph G.
'Guy' Thibodaux, Jr.
(1921-2016) was Chief of the Propulsion and Power Division at NASA's Manned Spacecraft Center (later Johnson Space Center), Houston, Texas between 1964 and 1980.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Hey, Jerry, it says in the Flight Plan to perform housekeeping chores.
We're having a devil of a time finding a housekeeper up here.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Who's the Junior man, Dick?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: He's on the Exer-Genie right now.
He thinks he made Captain already, but he's got a surprise.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger.
Your next midcourse looks like about a foot per second.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
So I guess maybe we won't need to do any of those, huh?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
Looks real good for here anyway.

Another little gem of wisdom we got for you here, when you entered PTC, you collapsed your deadband when you used Verb 37 Enter to get the P00, so just as a little helpful reminder, we want you to remember that when you come out, you're going to zap to your Noun 22 attitude as soon as you activate your jets unless you come out in SCS.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
Understand.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We figured you already knew it, but we wanted to show you how smart we were.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Good, thank you [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Houston, 12.
What do you think now?
I just redid a Verb 79.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Will that do it?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
You just cured it.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
I guess we called a P21 in there or something; we got curious to see how far out we were and that must have collapsed it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's affirmative, that's what you did.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 33 hours, and 9 minutes now into the flight Apollo 12.
At present the Apollo 12 spacecraft is 122,405.6 nautical miles [226,695.2 km] above the Earth.
Its velocity steadily decreasing now reads 4,098 feet per second [1,249 m/s].
Since our last status report of some 20 minutes ago we've had only brief conversations with the crew of Apollo 12...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
Dick, you listening?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: No, Dick's not on the horn.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
How about Al, is he listening?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: I'm listening, yes.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Okay, Al.
I checked home for you and Sue got back this afternoon from the Cape.
She said to say yesterday was a pretty exciting day for her, kind of wet, too.
And she wanted to let you know that she had attended a birthday dinner party that was honoring Vice President Agnew yesterday and had a real fine time.
She says her Mom and Dad are there with her at home now.
They just finished dinner, and the kids are headed for bed, and she wishes you Godspeed.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Thanks, Jerry.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Okay.
Pete, I guess Jane and the boys are...
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Dick's up now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, I guess Jane and the boys are out to dinner, I haven't been able to get a hold of them yet.
And Dick, you up?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay.
Barbara says that Barb and Norman and your mother are all there now, and everybody's doing fine, and they've had enough excitement for a while, thanks.
And if you don't mind, they just as soon you lay off the spectaculars.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Why?
What in the world has happened?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: [Laughter.] She said Aunt Dorothy is passing through Houston now, and she called in to say hello, and she said also to let you know that Bill Der Bing is there with them, and he's doing a great job.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Very good, thank you.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
The Mr.
Thibodaux who is thanked very early in that exchange by Pete Conrad is Mr.
Guy Thibodaux who is the Chief of the power and propulsion division at the Manned Spacecraft Center.
The reference was an obvious reference with regard to the performance of the engine on Midcourse Correction 2.
Bill Der Bing who is referred to later in Jerry Carr's report is also an MSC employee who is a member of the special events office of the Public Affairs.
At 33 hours, 15 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Hello, Houston, 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Say, you know, yesterday we talked to you about this stuff that's all over the windows, and on Dick's rendezvous window - window 2 - We've got some kind of a white deposit that's like a - I don't know, it's like an ash and it's not flush on it, it's built up in little humps and tabs of it sticking out, especially in the upper right-hand corner.
What we'd like to do is photograph this for you.
We don't have much to do right now.
We thought we'd photograph these windows and thought maybe you'd come up with some recommended settings and everything.
Seems to me I remember somewhere we had those procedures for photographing windows back in D or something because they were bad.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
We'll get somebody going on that right away.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We expect this stuff will be long gone after re-entry.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
We've been kind of dying to ask you too about the ice situation.
How long did it take that to sublime off?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Well, now, that's a funny thing.

The ice was sublimed off, but it's left a white deposit all along the perimeter of the number 1 window, that - that's on the black surface of the outside of that window, and it's up along the side of the window too.
It's similar to the same stuff that's on the rendezvous window.
Now, the ice that was on the inside of the outer pane in the number 1 window - that's sublimated, too, since we started PTC.

We have a - a fine deposit of water droplet; whatever was in the water - has adhered to the window and that's all in streaks and dots and splatters, so we kind of think some of this may have come when - when the tower was jettisoned; we're not sure, and I guess what we want to do is try and photograph this as best we can for you to see - because I haven't heard [garble] before either.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
How long did it take for that outer layer of ice on window 1 to sublime?
How long was it before it was gone?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Yes, it didn't go until we were finally asleep last night or - we stopped looking at it.
It was still there, I think, when we went to bed last night.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
The polls are closed now and - soon as we get enough election results to give you a meaningful information here, we will run you up some returns.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
I'm going to run to get a bite to eat right now and your friendly AFD is going to be monitoring the line for you.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Roger-Roger.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 33 hours, 29 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 12.
Our displays in Mission Control now show the Apollo 12 spacecraft at an altitude of 123,206 nautical miles [228,178 km] above the Earth and now traveling at 4,069 feet per second [1,240 m/s]...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
The election results referenced by CapCom, Jerry Carr, are from the Houston city elections.
We will try to pass these along to the crew sometimes later this evening and as you heard in that last comment, Jerry is now out of the Mission Operations Control Room grabbing a bit to eat.
At 33 hours, 34 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger.
I've got some words for you on the camera settings.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
Wait 1 [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay, Houston.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger.
Step number 1: make sure the Sun is incident to the window but not shining directly into the camera.
Step number 2: set the focus at 3 feet, and then do as best you can to hold your camera 3 feet from your target, realizing that this focal length is pretty sensitive to the distance.
Your shutter speed is 1/250th.
And if you don't have the spotmeter out, use an f-stop of 5.6 and take a picture of each window, and then go to an f-stop of 4 and take one of each window.
And if you do have the spotmeter out and want to use that, then the only caution here is to be sure that the spotmeter is focused on the window or the target itself.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Understand, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston, with some election news.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay.
About 10 per cent of the votes are in and counted now, and Mayor Louie Welch is leading his nearest contender, who is Curtis Graves, by 10,000 votes to 3,000; these are approximates.
In the six City Council positions that are up for grabs, all the incumbents are leading at this time, and the housing code question in Houston is running about 8,800 for and 6,200 against.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston.
The photo settings passed up were to allow the photography of the white deposits around the spacecraft windows.
That was Al Bean talking with Capsule Communicator Jerry Carr.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've just picked up another conversation with the Apollo 12 crew and we'll play that for you very shortly.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston at 34 hours, and 4 minutes now into the flight.
The Apollo 12 spacecraft is presently 124,563 nautical miles away from earth.
Its velocity, continuing to decrease and now reading 4,022 feet per second ...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We have a - some little bits of information here on the P23 that you've done so far.
Also, it's generated a few questions that we'd like to ask.
The first batch that you did showed a Delta-H of 49.2 kilometers, and the second batch showed 24.8 kilometers, and the marks that you took in each batch, in each set, were consistent.
And the uncertainty both times was about 4 kilometers, so we've got four questions to ask you which might possibly help us decide which Delta-H to use.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: One of the functions of Dick's initial navigation exercises is to calibrate exactly where on Earth's horizon he is measuring star angles from.
Due to the atmosphere, Earth's limb is not sharply defined but instead is rather fuzzy.
The CMP's trained at a specialised simulator at MIT to choose a consistent point in that limb to mark on.

Then on the actual flight, and knowing from ground-based tracking where they actually are, the engineers could work out the difference between the true limb and the height in the atmosphere the CMP was marking on.
This is the Delta-H that Carr refers to.
It is notable that Carr states these heights in kilometres which reflects MIT's decision to carry out navigation and tracking using metric units.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay.
First question.
Did you notice any difference between the horizons on the two batches; in other words, did you select a new horizon the second time?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Abso - absolutely, on the first one, they used top of the haze layer; on the second one, the haze layer was hardly discernible, used - what considered the true Earth horizon.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay, Dick, and the second question is - well, you've answered the second question essentially.
We wanted to know if you used the same technique, or did you put the star below or above the upper part of the horizon, but that's answered.
Next, is - were the stars...
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Well, Jerry, the reason I...
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Go ahead, Dick.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: The reason I did that, the second batch, that haze layer was hardly discernible at all, and the Earth's sphere was so sharp at that point, that I used it.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay then, Dick.
Then, I guess you're saying then that you liked the second batch better than the first hatch.
Is that affirmed?
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay.
Did you get my last question, Dick?
Is - I'm assuming that you liked the second batch better than the first batch then.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That's affirmative, it was much sharper, much easier to define the horizon on the second batch.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger, Dick.
We kind of got the impression that the altitude seemed to be a function of the star of magnitude and we're wondering, were the star images pretty well focused?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That's affirmative.
I didn't seem to notice that.

The only thing I can say about the first batch is that they were at the very top of the - of the haze layer where it just starts to turn that very light blue, and I thought it was a lot further away than I expected it to be.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger, Dick.
And last question, was there any noticeable stray light and was it different on each batch?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: No, the optics appeared to be excellent, they didn't have any problem with stray light whatsoever.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Okay.
Thank you, Dick.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston.
That was Dick Gordon critiquing with Jerry Carr, the Program 23, the star sighting navigation program onboard the spacecraft.
We're now at 34 hours, 12 minutes into the flight and we show Apollo 12 registering at an altitude of 124,872 nautical miles [231,263 km].
Its velocity now reads 4,011 feet per second [1,223 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Go ahead, 12.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We've been having a little discussion here on this photograph in the windows.
We had the exterior color - I presume, really what you wanted was the CEX - HCEX rather than the normal color.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Stand by a second, Pete; I'll check on it.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Well - We were showing for the color exterior, the 368, that we should have shot at a much slower speed, so that prompted us to think you meant us to use the HCEX, and so maybe you'd better clarify that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
They're checking right now.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
While we're looking for that answer for you, I've got an LOI minus 5 flyby PAD - maneuver PAD for you if you want to grab out a form.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
Wait one.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
We're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
LOI minus 5 flyby, SPS/G&N: 62633, Noun 48, plus 0.90, minus 0.17; Noun 33, 078:27:16.61, Noun 81, plus 0068.4, minus 0206.0, plus 0579.3; roll, pitch, and yaw is 052, 217, 338; Noun 44 is NA, NA.
Delta-VT0618.6, 1:26, 0613.6; sextant 32, 184.6, 38.6; boresight is NA, NA, NA; Noun 61, plus 07.38, minus 169.95, 1165.9, 36231; GET 0.5 g's, 145:52:20; GDC Align stars are Sirius and Rigel.
Roll is 256, pitch 152, yaw 069.
Ullage, none.
Other burn is SPS docked.
The LM weight is 33585.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Roger.

LOI minus 5, SPS/G&N, and I may have screwed up on this [garble] 62633, plus 0.90, minus 0.17, plus 078:27:16.61; Noun 81, plus 0068.4, minus 0206.0, plus 0579.3; roll, pitch, and yaw 052, 217, 338; Noun 44, NA, NA.
The stars Sirius and Rigel, roll 256, 152, 069, ullage none, SPS docked, LM weight 33585.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's affirmative, 12.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That was Commander Pete Conrad taking down the T minus 5 hour...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: A full interpretation of the PAD follows:Purpose:This PAD is for a contingency burn, in case the crew will not be able to enter lunar orbit.
Instead, the burn would set the spacecraft on a precise trajectory for a return to Earth after going around the Moon.

It would take place some 5 hours before the planned time of lunar orbit insertion.Propulsion/guidance (PROP/GUID):The burn would be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM weight (Noun 47):62,633 pounds (28,410 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):+0.90° and -0.17°.

These are the angles away from the longitudinal axis the SPS engine bell will be set to for the burn.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):78 hours, 27 minutes, 16.61 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):x, +68.2 (+20.8); y, -206.0 (-62.8); z, +579.3 (+176.6).
These velocity components are expressed with respect to the local vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 52°; Pitch, 217°; Yaw, 338°.

The attitude figure is with respect to the IMU orientation at the time of the burn.HA, expected apocynthion of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.HP, expected pericynthion of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.Delta-VT:618.6 fps (188.5 m/s).
This is essentially a vector sum of the above velocities and as such represents the total velocity change.Burn duration or burn time:1 minute, 26 seconds.Delta-VC:613.6 fps.
This is the fps value that will be entered into the EMS to give it backup control of the burn.
The value is smaller than Delta-VTbecause the EMS cannot compensate for the tail-off impulse generated by the engine after the cut-off command.

By entering a lower number, (and should the G&N system fail to shut the engine down) the burn would be stopped early enough by the EMS, that the extra thrust from the tail-off would bring the final Delta-V to the required value.Sextant star:Star 32 (Alphecca, Alpha Coronae Borealis) should be visible in the sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are ° and ° respectively.
This is one of the attitude checks the crew will make prior to the burn.Boresight star:Not applicable.
(This refers to a star that will be visible in the Crew Optical Alignment Sight, a portable monocular telescope, when mounted onto the Commander's window in a prescribed angle.
This is an additional attitude check independent of the Inertial Measurement Unit-calculated attitude.

Usually it is not carried out if the LM or a planet/moon is obscuring the windows.Boresight Star pitch angle on COAS:Not applicable.Boresight Star X position on COAS:Not applicable.Expected splashdown point (Noun 61):7.38° north , 169.95° west; in the Mid-Pacific.
.Range to go at the 0.05 g event:1,165.9 nautical miles.
(2,159.3 km) To set up their EMS (Entry Monitor System) before re-entry, the crew need to know the expected distance the CM would travel from the 0.05g event to landing.
This figure will be decremented by the EMS based on signals from its own accelerometer.Expected velocity at the 0.05g event:36,231 fps.
(11,043 m/s) This is another entry for the EMS.
It is entered into the unit's Delta-V counter and will be decremented based on signals from its own accelerometer.GET of 0.05g event:Expected at 145 hours, 52 minutes and 20 seconds GET.

This is when it is expected that the EMS and P64 will be triggered.GDC Align stars:Stars 15 (Sirius, Alpha Canis Majoris) and 12 (Rigel, Beta Orionis) to be used for backup GDC Align in case the IMU is unavailable for this task.GDC Align angles:256°, 152° and 69°.Other notes include that the SPS propellant tanks are full, so there would be no need to perform an ullage burn to settle their contents.
The burn details assume the LM is still docked and the LM weight, or mass, is 33,585 pounds-mass or 15,234 kg.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston; 12.
Do you read?

Your read back was correct.

Very good.
[Long pause.]

transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.

tokens: Roger.
Pete, you guessed us right.
The data we sent you was based on HCEX film.
So that is what you ought to use.

Thank you.

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 34 hours, 33 minutes since lift-off.
Apollo 12 is now 125,686 nautical miles [232,770 km] away from Earth.
Velocity now reads 3,982 feet per second [1,214 m/s].
We've had discussions with the crew who asked about the type of film to be used in their window photography.
Additionally, we passed up one of the contingency PADs to them for storage in their computer...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The flyby PAD just read up will not be stored in the computer.
The machine has not been designed as an information storage device.
Rather, having written the information onto a P30 PAD form, it exists on paper to be used if required.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

Can we put our headsets back on now?

You might consider taking a stereo pair with that camera on the window.

tokens: Okay.
We'll give her a try.

tokens: Long comm break.

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
You heard the reference from Jerry Carr jokingly saying can we put our headsets back on.

At 34 hours, 42 minutes of the flight, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Go ahead, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Carr

transcript.speaker: Conrad

transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
Very good.[Pause.]

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Pete.
If you're looking for something to do, we got a geology pop quiz for you.

tokens: [Laugh] Oh, thanks a lot.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Go ahead, give it to them.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Long comm break.

tokens: Houston, 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: We think we have the S-IVB in sight.
We've - had a - an object which is in the same place all the time and appears to be tumbling.
We've had it ever since yesterday, and it just seems to be tagging along with us, so I guess that's the S-IVB.
It's usually out our center hatch window when our roll angle is about 35 degrees right now.
Maybe that'll give you a clue, and somebody can figure out if that's what we've really looking at.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Very long comm break.

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 34 hours, 56 minutes now into the flight.
Apollo 12 is presently at altitude of 126,547 nautical miles [234,365 km].
Its velocity now reads 3,953 feet per second [1,205 m/s]...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
That was Jerry Carr offering Pete Conrad and Al Bean a geology pop quiz if they wanted something to do and we literally could do that in Mission Control Center now.
Astronaut/Geologist, Jack Schmitt has joined Jerry Carr in the Control Center and in fact was a source of inspiration for Jerry Carr's offering.
You heard Dick Gordon, Command Module Pilot, who will not go to the lunar surface, suggest that we give them the quiz.
That was Pete Conrad who reported sighting an object out the window, which he thought, might be the third stage of the Saturn V launch vehicle.
At 34 hours, 59 minutes now in the flight of Apollo 12; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston at 35 hours and 29 minutes since lift-off.
We've had no voice contact or communications with the Apollo 12 crew since our last announcement.
However, we currently show an altitude of 127,808 nautical miles [236,700 km] for Apollo 12.
Its velocity now reads 3,910 feet per second [1,192 m/s].
So at 35 hours, 29 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston continuing to monitor.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; at 36 hours, 6 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 12.
Since our last communication, we have not attempted to contact the crew nor have they called the Mission Control Center.
Our present altitude, as shown on our displays, reads 129,204 nautical miles [239,286 km] above the Earth.
Velocity now reading 3,862 feet per second [1,177 m/s] for Apollo 12.
So at this time we continue with a very quite phase of the mission and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 12,Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
That thing you saw off the hatch, at a roll of 35 degrees, we figured there's probably three possible answers.
Number 1: it could be the S-IVB, or possibly a SLA panel, or it could be the backup crew flying trail on you.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Roger.
Actually we have two objects out there.
One's not anywhere near as bright as the other, so I think the real bright one's the S-IVB and the other one's probably a SLA panel.
They're about 20 degrees apart.
And as far as the backup crew goes, tell them we'll meet them on the back side of the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.

Best as we can tell down here now - best as we can tell down here now, the S-IVB should be near Denebola, and if it's SLA panels - correction, what you are saying now ought to be near Enif; is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Yes, it's near Enif.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
And the words here are that the S-IVB is about 180 degrees away, near Denebola.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
I wonder what that could be.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
We'll go back to our drawing board.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: The object's very bright - The object's very bright, and it's obviously something that's tumbling.
It's tumbling about 1½ rev's per second, or at least it's flashing at us about that.
And Dick - Dick is going to tell you what star it's near.
He's messing with his chart right now.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
We're standing by.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, as best as we can tell, looking at things down here, on those SLA panels, we assume that they weren't imparted any great amount of Delta-V, like anything more than 1 or so feet per second when they separated.
Your SLA panels would probably only be about 300 miles away from you right now.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: That could be true but, gee whiz, when we turned around, I saw one of those SLA panels leaving the area at a high rate of speed; it looked to me like it was leaving us pretty - pretty rapid clip, like it got a lot more than a foot per second or so.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Well, since we don't really have any idea how they left or what their trajectory could be, it's kind of tough really to say just what the heck that could be.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
We'll assume it's friendly anyway, okay?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger.
If it makes any noises, it's probably just wind in the rigging.
transcript.speaker: Carr

tokens: Okay.
Understand.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 36 hours, 36 minutes into the flight of Apollo 12.
Apollo 12 is presently 130,289 nautical miles [241,295 km] above the Earth.
It's velocity now reading 3,825 feet per second [1,166 m/s].
Capsule communicator Jerry Carr has been passing along to Apollo 12 some of the Mission Operations Control Room insights as to the tumbling object, which the crew reported seeing earlier.
We will play that conversation to you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston.
So we have no definite agreement as to what the crew may have sighted.
Our best estimates place the S-IVB to be about 2,500 nautical miles [4,600 km] away from Apollo 12 at this time.
The SLA panels that were not attached at the time of the S-IVB as slingshot maneuver.
A smiling Dave Scott was in the control room when they remarked about the back up crew following was made.
That was Dick Gordon from the spacecraft who makes the assumption that whatever they saw must be friendly.
At 36 hours, 40 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 37 hours, 26 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 12.
The Apollo 12 spacecraft is now 132,125 nautical miles [244,696 km] away from Earth.
It's velocity now reads 3,763.7 feet per second [1,147.2 m/s].
Since our last announcement, we have had no further contact with the Apollo 12 crew and quiet studied atmosphere predominates in the Mission Control Center.

Although you saw the midcourse correction burn performed on television earlier today, and knew that it was performed as advertised, we will pass along the official statistics from the Flight Dynamics Officer's log.
The Time Of Ignition for that burn was 30 hours, 52 minutes and 43 seconds Ground Elapsed Time.
Its Delta velocity was 61.8 feet per second [18.8 m/s] and burn duration was 8.82 seconds.

Although the requirement for additional midcourse corrections is considered very slight at this time the Flight Dynamics Officer will continue to evaluate his vectors and the option will remain open at least for the time being.
At 37 hours, 28 minutes into the flight of Apollo 12; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, 12.

Go.

Go.

We're getting close enough to the Moon now so that, even with the - the monocular, the Moon looks a lot like these photographs that you see taken from many of the observatories that are around the center.
We were looking at it through the sextant a minute ago, and it really looks spectacular.
It's starting to take on less of a silver color and more of a gray color when you look at it up close.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Roger, Al.
Watch those color descriptions.

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We're getting a bit of noisy communications here as the spacecraft rotates through one Omni antenna and on to the next.
The change of shift is presently going on in Mission Control.
Flight Director Clifford Charlesworth is coming on to replace Flight Director Pete Frank and his team of flight controllers.
The capsule communicator on this shift will be astronaut Ed Gibson replacing astronaut Jerry Carr.
At 38 hours, 10 minutes into the flight of Apollo 12, the spacecraft is now 133,689 nautical miles [247,592 km] from Earth, traveling at a speed of some 3,700 feet per second [1,130 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 39 hours, 10 minutes.
Apollo 12 presently at an altitude of 135,809 nautical miles [251,518 km] from Earth, traveling at a speed of 3,642 feet per second [1,110 m/s].
We've had no further conversations with the crew since our last report.
Flight Director Clifford Charlesworth went around the room and polled each of his flight controllers for a status shortly after coming on shift.
The spacecraft continues to function normally and no problems.
The crew is scheduled to begin a 10-hour rest period at 43 hours into the mission or about 4 hours from now.
The spacecraft is continuing to maintain a stable Passive Thermal Control attitude, rotating at a rate of about 3 revolutions per hour.
At 39 hours, 11 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Comm break.

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Hello, troops.
Looks like a good burn today.
Good show.
Say, Pete, we got some folks sitting next to us here who would like to get some Biomed data on the three of you tonight.
Why don't you kick it around and give us a reply?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: What do you want us to do?
Keep our Biomed hooked up tonight?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's affirm.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay.
You want it on all three?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's correct.
One thing they'd like to do is to get a baseline to help them during the EVA.
They'd like to get - Apparently they use some of that data for input to the equations which they'll be using during the EVA.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Sleep - stuff?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's what we're told.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay.
We'll leave it hooked up unless it bothers us.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: I'm not exactly sure how you get it inside the sleeping bag.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
That sounds good.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston; Apollo 12.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Hello, 12; Houston.
Go ahead.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Hello, 12; Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Hello, Houston; Apollo 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go ahead.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Hello, 12; Houston.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Roger.
The LM/CSM Delta-P is plus 1.7.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We copy.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And we'll be bringing the purge line heaters on at 40 plus 35 per the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: And what do you want us to dump the waste water to - 25 per cent or 15 per cent?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Let's take it down to 10 per cent this time.
That'll give you a pretty good margin.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Roger.
Ten per cent.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 40 hours, 2 minutes.
Apollo 12 now traveling at a speed of 3,585 feet per second [1,093 m/s] and 137,600 nautical miles [254,835 km] from Earth.
We're in conversation with the spacecraft at this time...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You folks have been pretty quiet.
What's up?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Nothing.
We're just exercising and listening to the tape recorder and looking at the Moon and looking at the Earth - or reading the books.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
How's that tape recorder?
Is the RPM up to nominal?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Yes.
It works pretty good here in zero g.
It works better than it does down there on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: It just doesn't have much in the way of volume up here with a 5 psi.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: How's that cloud cover over the Pacific at this time?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, Australia is real clear again, and it doesn't look quite as cloudy north of Australia.
It's sort of a - It's got a rectangular shape to it, whatever the system is north of Australia there.
It's kind of funny looking.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
What's the smallest piece of land you can pick out?
Can you see any of the Pacific islands?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: [Laughter] No, we haven't - We can't see any of them.
One of - Dick just says he can see Borneo right now.
He's looking through the monocular.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You can't see it with your naked eye, though.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: I think one of the problems is that - The only thing that we could see would be close to the curve.
All the - the terminator - We're not looking at that much of an Earth and all the water in the Pacific that's close to the terminator has a very shallow Sun angle on it, and it's fairly washed out.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
You still getting a pretty good glint off the surface then?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Yes.
Dick says the subsolar point is just west of Australia.
By the way, how far out are we now?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
The board's showing a little over 140,000 - Stand by.
We'll give you a little more accurate.[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay.[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, you're now 137,720 [nautical miles, 255,057 km] out, and you're going along at 3,580 feet per second [1,091 m/s].
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Roger.[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
We're going to ask the DSKY and see what it thinks.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Not bad.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
We're showing...
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: ...[garble]...
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: ...755 right now.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: It'll only read to the nearest tenths, and the velocity is 3,579.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
We're just about the same as you, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 12, Houston.
We have some unofficial results from the elections today.
It looks as though Mayor Louie Welch was re-elected to a fourth term.
His closest competitor, Curtis Graves, got about 59,000, and the mayor's up to 98 now.
According to - that's the first time since, I believe, 1927 we got a fourth term for a mayor.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Very good.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Say, Al, is that Moon beginning to look a little bigger to you now?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: It sure is.
We were watching it through the sextant a while ago, and the features on the Moon are much more pronounced than they are - than we've seen before we left Earth.
They looked almost like one of these strip pictures you see from an Earth-based telescope.
The terminator is about 7 or 8 degrees east, and it's right on the edge of a maria and running through some of the terra, and it's very stark and beautiful from this point of view.
I imagine tomorrow it's going to be even more impressive.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Probably be very impressive from a distance of around 3 or 4 feet.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That, too.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We're now about 3½ hours from the scheduled beginning of the crew sleep period.
It continues to be quite quiet, both from the spacecraft and from here in Mission Control.
The crew scheduled to begin a eat period at about 41 hours, 30 minutes; about an hour from now.
Spacecraft presently traveling at a speed of 3,561 feet per second [1,085 m/s], has reached an altitude of 138,330 nautical miles [256,187 km] from Earth.
At 40 hours, 23 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: 12, if you hold off on the waste water dump and the fuel cell purge until 41:20, you'll minimize the effect on PTC.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Sounds like a good suggestion.
We'll do that.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 40 hours, 58 minutes into the flight of Apollo 12.
The spacecraft now traveling at speed of 3,523 feet per second [1,074 m/s] and an altitude up to 139,509 nautical miles [258,371 km].
Continues to be very quiet here this morning.

We have had one brief conversation with the crew since our last report requesting that they hold off on the scheduled waste water dump until about 41:20:00 at which time will be any more favorable position from standpoint of the Passive Thermal Control, and have the minimum impact on the Passive Thermal Control...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Houston.
Apollo 12 is dumping waste water and purging fuel cells.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, 12.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Pete, can you see any ice crystals from that?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: You can see the water dumps; there's no doubt about that.
There's all kinds of it.
In fact, they look like a regular snowstorm.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Does the snowstorm tend to hang around or does it move off pretty quickly?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: It moves out pretty fast.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston; 12.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Hey; Roger, Ed.
I don't think that water dump did that PTC any good at all.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: G&C concurs with that down here.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Dick, let us watch it a little while.
We haven't seen you - much travel since you made that dump.
You're around 25 degrees out now.
We'll be keeping an eye on it and see which way you're going.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 41 hours, 50 minutes.
Apollo 12 now traveling at a speed of 3,467 feet per second [1,057 m/s] up to an altitude of 141,270 nautical miles [261,632 km].
The crew advised us a short while ago that they were performing the waste water dump and fuel cell purge as listed on the Flight Plan.

A little while after that Dick Gordon reported that apparently the energy imparted by dumping the waste water had affected the Passive Thermal Control attitude apparently setting up some sort of a mild rate in the slow rotational rate that we have.

We're watching that in Mission Control now to see if it's going to be necessary for the crew to damp out the movements about the spacecraft axes and reestablish the Passive Thermal Control before they go to sleep.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Go ahead, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: In looking at your - your angles here, it looks as though you might be pretty touch and go during the sleep period if you don't go ahead and re-initialize the PTC, and when you do that, give us a little better - a little better propellant margins.
We'd like you to go ahead and reinitialize the DAP to 0.2 degrees per second rather than the 0.5 you have in there now.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Got you.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
As you heard, CapCom Ed Gibson advised the crew to re-establish the Passive Thermal Control mode.
This is primarily because of the wobbling motions imparted to the rotational rate of the spacecraft by the water dump.

It's felt that if the wobble is allowed to continue during the sleep period, it could conceivably grow to the point during the night where it would be necessary to awaken the crew to damp out this wobbling movement.

Rather than take that chance and perhaps have to awaken the crew during the night, we've elected to go ahead and re-establish the Passive Thermal Control mode after stabilizing the spacecraft and the roll rate.
And the new Passive Thermal Control mode will be about 2 revolutions per hour.
The purpose of this, of course, is to maintain the proper temperature balances within the spacecraft as the vehicle rotates putting first one side and then the other toward the sunlight.
Apollo 12 now 34 - out of 141,562 nautical miles [262,173 km] from Earth and traveling at a speed of 3,457 feet per second [1,054 m/s].
At 41 hours, 59 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 12, Houston.
We've got a state vector for you, if you'll give us Accept.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 12, Houston.
The state vector is in, and we're ready for the E-Mod, whenever you want.
No hurry.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: State vector's in; the computer is yours.
We're also ready to pick up on the roll now, Dick; and we're ready for the E-Mod dump, no hurry on that.
Also, we have a question; have you cycled the water quantity indicator switch recently?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Yes, which one do you want it in?
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Your position - I guess you have it in Waste right now - is fine.
No problem either way, we just wanted to know whether you cycled it because we saw a funny down here.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Well, it's in Potable right now, do you want it in Waste?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Affirmative, Pete.
Waste is good.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: What was the funny?
Okay.
It's in Waste, and what was the funny?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Pete, we saw the water quantity go from 101 per cent down to 72 per cent and remain there for a period of time and then work it's way back on up to 101 per cent, and if you had cycled that switch, that would do it.
That's the reason for the question.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay.
I don't really remember, but we may have cycled it a couple of times.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: E-memory dump coming at you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger.
We're standing by.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 42 hours, 29 minutes.
Apollo 12 now 142,546 nautical miles [263,995 km] from Earth; 3,426 feet per second [1,044 m/s].
The crew is presently going through their pre-sleep checklist, getting the spacecraft prepared for the sleep period.
They are scheduled to begin an eat period at about this time and then begin their rest cycle - a 10-hour rest period at 43 hours, or about 30 minutes from now.
We have had a couple of brief conversations with the crew since our last report.
Pete Conrad advised a short while ago that they were going to dump the erasable memory for a routine check.
The pre-sleep check list also [garble] plus chlorinating the onboard drinking water and setting a number of switches in the proper positions prior to beginning the sleep period.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 12, Houston.
We have the E-Mod.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: 12, Roger.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, it appears we will have no further conversation, at least for the time being.
I probably will hear from the crew at least once more before they begin their sleep period.
Probably within about 30 minutes.
The spacecraft continues to function normally, all systems normal.
We are now 142,814 nautical miles [264,492 km] from Earth, traveling at a speed of 3,417 feet per second [1,042 m/s].
At 42 hours and 37 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, Apollo 12.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: 12, Houston.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Roger.
Let me give you the onboard readouts: Bat Charlie, 37.0; pyro Bat A, 37.0; pyro Bat B, 37.1.
We have no RCS quantities.
The indicator is on Main A.
Pre-sleep checklist is complete, and we'll be going to our Comm configurations for sleep here in just a minute.
And we're still doing some housekeeping chores, so you'll see some Biomed on and off, a little bit, while we get ready to go to bed.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Roger, Pete.
We copy.
And we have the RCS consumables if you're interested.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay.
Ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And that's at 40 hours even.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And we are assuming a negative crew status report.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: That's right.[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 43 hours, 4 minutes.
The crew just called in and reported that they have completed the pre-sleep check list, and will be beginning their sleep period shortly.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 12, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Before you go on off to sleep, would you take the - clear the DSKY and do it with a Verb 45 or Verb 66 rather than 37?
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Is there any reason why we can't leave the clock time up there?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Yes.
We just prefer, although it's not a strong desire, to clear the DSKY so you're not working the relays in the DSKY, give you a little longer life.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And, 12, your PTC is looking good; we expect less than 6 degrees excursion during the sleep.
transcript.speaker: Gibson

tokens: 043:xx:xx Gibson:And 12, we'll be talking to you tomorrow.
So long.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew sleep period is scheduled to last some 10 hours, beginning at about 43 hours as scheduled in the Flight Plan.
Apollo 12 is presently 143,833 nautical miles [266,379 km] from the Earth.
The spacecraft velocity down now to 3,386 feet per second [1,032 m/s].
At 43 hours, 9 minutes into the flight; this is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO
